title: Blog 1
content:
SYSTEM MONITORING ON AWS: STREAMING CLOUDWATCH METRICS TO OPENTELEMETRY COLLECTOR IN VPC USING LAMBDA Managing observability for large-scale cloud systems has always been a challenging problem, especially when enterprises want to balance cost, flexibility, and security. Although OpenTelemetry is gradually becoming the common standard helping organizations escape vendor lock-in, they face a significant technical barrier: how to stream metrics from AWS CloudWatch directly into internal collection systems strictly isolated within a VPC? To solve this problem, a solution using AWS Lambda as a data relay station from CloudWatch Metric Streams to the internal network has proven its outstanding effectiveness, ensuring data flows are always transmitted securely and in near real-time.
KEY HIGHLIGHTS OF THIS PUSH-BASED OBSERVABILITY SOLUTION: Shifting from Pull to Push model: Using tools like Prometheus to continuously “pull” (poll) data causes API throttling, metric loss, and high costs. The “Push” model with CloudWatch Metric Streams completely solves this problem, enabling event-driven data transmission with sub-minute latency for real-time alerting.
Overcoming Amazon Data Firehose limitations: Although Firehose supports pushing data to HTTP endpoints, it requires those to be public endpoints. To deliver data into a private subnet, AWS uses a Lambda Transform Function as a bridge. Firehose batches the data and invokes Lambda; then Lambda pushes metrics through the NLB deep into the VPC securely.
OpenTelemetry Collector is the heart of the system: Running on Amazon EC2 instances inside the VPC, the Collector operates as a processing hub with 3 flexible components: Receivers (data ingestion), Processors (filtering, enriching data, masking sensitive data), and Exporters (sending data to diverse destinations like Grafana, AWS X-Ray, or Amazon OpenSearch).
Scalability and safe failover: By using Network Load Balancer to distribute TCP traffic to EC2 instances, the system can easily scale horizontally. Additionally, an Amazon S3 bucket is configured as a failover destination for Firehose (though no storage costs are incurred if Lambda successfully pushes the data).
Cost optimization and eliminating Vendor Lock-in: Using OpenTelemetry (Apache 2.0 license, completely free) helps enterprises eliminate the burden of third-party software licensing costs. Its standardization allows operations teams to freely switch monitoring backend platforms later without rebuilding the data collection pipeline.
Solution Architecture Diagram Overall, the clever combination of AWS services such as Lambda, Firehose, and Network Load Balancer has created a perfect bridge, closing the gap between public cloud services and closed internal networks. This architecture not only thoroughly addresses the latency and limitations of traditional APIs but also empowers technical teams to the fullest. By establishing an intelligent data transmission pipeline, enterprises can freely build a powerful, independent monitoring ecosystem that is budget-optimized and always ready to scale in the future without worrying about technology dependency risks.
Building a monitoring system is not hard — the challenge is making it secure, cost-effective, and technology-independent. What do you think about this Push-based architecture with Lambda and OpenTelemetry? If you are currently operating systems, is your biggest barrier the security story (getting metrics into a private VPC safely) or the infrastructure cost optimization problem? Let’s discuss!
Article link: https://www.facebook.com/groups/awsstudygroupfcj/permalink/2173544653410495/?rdid=CUzsR7GSYpgaIwjW
Reference guide: https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/architecture/streaming-cloudwatch-metrics-to-vpc-based-opentelemetry-collectors-using-lambda/

title: Introduction
content:
5.1. Introduction This lab provides detailed instructions on how to comprehensively deploy the Resume Fit system on the AWS cloud platform. Resume Fit is an AI application that helps quickly and transparently evaluate the compatibility between candidate profiles (CVs) and job descriptions (JDs) to support recruitment decisions.
The system is designed following standard architecture on AWS, ensuring high scalability and availability. Core components include: Isolated Network (VPC), Flexible Compute Servers (EC2, Auto Scaling Group), Relational Database (RDS PostgreSQL), Object Storage (S3), Asynchronous Processing Queue (SQS), Monitoring Service (CloudWatch), and Secrets Management (Secrets Manager).
Architecture Diagram Resume Fit AWS Architecture Overview

title: Event 1
content:
Harvest Report: “FCAJ Community Day” Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Day Event Date: May 9, 2026 Location: 26th Floor, Bitexco Financial Tower, No. 02 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City Organizers: AWS Study Group in collaboration with the FCAJ Community Event Objectives Program Goal: To create an environment and opportunity for young individuals (especially 3rd and 4th-year students or fresh graduates) to confidently share knowledge, practice public speaking, build self-confidence, and receive career orientation. Key Messages: Dopamine hacking for study motivation, Prompt Engineering optimization techniques, core mindset for IT professionals in the GenAI era, and standard software development workflows using AI (the BMAD methodology). Value for Attendees: Help attendees overcome the fear of being replaced by AI, understand the paramount importance of foundational knowledge, learn how to leverage tools through structured workflows, and cultivate core career values like integrity and long-term thinking. Speaker List Speaker 1: Khang Nguyen – Solution Architect at CloudKinetics Speaker 2: Thinh Nguyen – DevOps Engineer | FCAJ Ambassador at AWS Speaker 3: Long Hoang – FCAJ Ambassador at AWS Speaker 4: Thao Nguyen Phuong – Cloud Engineer Intern at VIB | FCJ Ambassador at AWS Event Highlights Problem Overview: Students and professionals often lack learning motivation and are easily distracted by social media due to a lack of “quick rewards”. Additionally, in professional environments, over-relying on AI to write code directly without context generates bloated, disconnected code (due to AI hallucinations). Solutions Introduced: To study effectively: break goals down, set up short-term rewards, and build a streak (gamification) to trick the brain into releasing dopamine. To master AI: focus on “foundations”, break tasks down into documentation templates (under the BMAD method) before allowing AI to generate code. Technologies, Services, & Tools: AWS Services: CloudFront, S3 (storage), Cognito (auth), API Gateway, Lambda (serverless backend), Bedrock (AI models), DynamoDB (NoSQL Database), CloudWatch (monitoring). AI Tools: LLMs (Claude, Gemini), “Promptimizer” browser extension, BMAD framework. Demos & Case Studies: Presented a real-world Serverless and AI-powered architecture built on AWS. Demoed a browser extension named “Promptimizer” used to highlight text and instantly optimize prompts inside the browser. Key Takeaway: AI acts as an amplifier of user capabilities: if your foundation is weak, it amplifies mistakes; if your foundation is solid, it boosts productivity exponentially. Key Learnings Mindset & Methodology: Use AI to assist in brainstorming, but never outsource your own understanding. Always ask “Why” for every technical decision. Technical Knowledge: Effective Prompt Engineering consists of: Role, Instruction, Context, Input, Output Format, and Constraints. Best Practices: To prevent AI context bloat, feed it incremental inputs instead of a single giant prompt. In development, let AI play distinct roles (BM, Architect, Dev, Scrum Master) handling small, well-reviewed stories rather than handing over the entire project. Real-world Insights: Recruiters do not just look for the correct final output; they evaluate the candidate’s thought process and the reasoning behind their chosen solutions. Experience is not measured by time, but by the “richness of challenges” handled across different projects. Application to My Work Current Project Applicability: Transform software development with AI by investing heavily in writing detailed technical documentation (VOD, System Architecture) first. Break projects down into Epics and Stories before having AI agents generate code and auto-review errors. Technologies to Explore: Adopt AWS Lambda and Amazon Cognito to build serverless backends without managing physical servers or user password databases. Use the “Promptimizer” extension to improve prompt quality. Workflow Improvement Ideas: Apply the 2-minute rule: if a task takes less than 2 minutes to resolve, do it immediately without delay. Event Experience Speaker Insights: Understood the 5 career pillars: Salary, Experience, Network, Knowledge, and Profile. Hands-on Experience: Reviewing system design case studies and watching live Promptimizer demos provided highly practical and vivid insights. Networking: The community strongly encourages members (including students) to speak up without fear of making mistakes or sharing things others might already know, emphasizing teamwork to go far. Most Memorable Aspect: The concept of Integrity—proactively handling corner cases and delivering high-quality work regardless of whether it is inspected, building trust for the long haul. Key Takeaways Most Important Concept: Master foundational knowledge; only when you understand the root of the problem can you accurately assess whether AI-generated outputs are correct or hallucinated. Real-world Experience: Making mistakes is essential for growth, especially as a fresher. Companies are willing to pay for your mistakes as long as you maintain a learning mindset and improve. Future Development Plan: Stay updated with new AWS announcements and tech trends to build structured knowledge. Strive to become a speaker who shares knowledge rather than a passive consumer of content. Build a resilient and persistent learning routine without expecting overnight results. Selected Event Photos Speaker sharing about Prompt Engineering
Presenting development methodologies with AI Agents
Sharing on Growth Mindset

title: Blog 2
content:
Practical Experience: Building an AI Gateway to Govern Amazon Bedrock with API Gateway Hi everyone. Recently, while working on AWS infrastructure labs—from configuring networks, troubleshooting security with Secrets Manager in Cloud9, to planning a machine learning integration with Prometheus for system monitoring—I faced a rather difficult problem: How to manage access control and govern costs when multiple teams start sharing Large Language Models (LLMs) on Amazon Bedrock?
In practice, when building Generative AI applications, you cannot simply hand out the Bedrock API key for everyone to call directly. Costs would skyrocket, and tenant isolation between users would be practically non-existent. After struggling for a while, I found an excellent reference pattern from Dynatrace and decided to test it out. Today, I’ll share my experience designing this “AI Gateway” from a practical perspective.
Architectural Overview - Light but Powerful Instead of letting clients access Bedrock directly, we place a shield with Amazon API Gateway at the center. The entire data flow is structured as follows (comparable to the solution diagram):
Route 53 (Optional but recommended): Instead of using AWS’s default long endpoint, we use Route 53 to map a custom domain for a clean and standardized corporate layout.
API Gateway (Main Entry Point): This is where we configure rate-limiting rules to prevent spam and allocate quotas to prevent budget overruns. A major benefit is its support for response streaming, ensuring real-time token streaming from LLMs works smoothly.
Lambda Authorizer (Security Checkpoint): API requests cannot be made anonymously. We configure a Lambda function to validate JWT tokens. Clients must present a valid token to proceed.
Lambda Integration (Heart of the System): This is my favorite part. This Lambda function acts as a diligent courier. It captures the original client request (headers, body, and query parameters), signs it using AWS Signature Version 4 (SigV4), and then forwards the call to Amazon Bedrock.
AI Gateway Solution Diagram Why I Highly Value This Pattern In practice, this model solves two core issues that infrastructure engineers care about deeply:
1. Transparent to Client I often test code locally and use the Boto3 library extensively. The beauty of this gateway is that client-side code requires almost no modifications. The external application acts as if it is interacting directly with Bedrock, while everything is silently governed behind a strict proxy layer.
2. Future-proof Design Anyone working on Cloud knows that AWS updates Bedrock features continuously. If the gateway had to hardcode every single API endpoint, every new model release would require code updates.
Since the Lambda Integration function simply packages and signs requests without parsing the API structure, if Bedrock introduces a new model tomorrow or updates its Knowledge Bases feature, the system will run smoothly without requiring any maintenance code changes.
Conclusion Having worked with monitoring and data visualization systems (like Grafana or Zabbix), consolidating all AI traffic into a single API Gateway makes it extremely easy to plug in monitoring tools to track system health and individual project costs.
Leveraging Serverless services to govern AI not only enhances security but also significantly optimizes operational overhead. If you are building infrastructure for GenAI applications on AWS, this AI Gateway model is definitely a piece to consider including in your architecture.
Article Link: https://www.facebook.com/groups/awsstudygroupfcj/permalink/2179637596134534/?rdid=654DT9MyMTrqQ5kg#
Reference Guide: https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/architecture/building-an-ai-gateway-to-amazon-bedrock-with-amazon-api-gateway/

title: Network Infrastructure
content:
5.2. Network Infrastructure (Networking) 5.2.1. Create VPC and Internet Gateway An isolated network environment (VPC) is a solid security foundation for the entire application, allowing full control over network traffic.
Navigate to the VPC Console and select Create VPC. Initialize a VPC named ResumeMatching-VPC and configure the IPv4 CIDR range as 10.0.0.0/16. Enable the Enable DNS Hostnames feature so that the VPC can resolve domain names. Create an Internet Gateway named ResumeMatching-IGW to provide outbound internet connectivity for public resources. Attach the newly created Internet Gateway to ResumeMatching-VPC. 5.2.2. Configure Subnets and Route Tables Divide the VPC IP range into subnets to isolate the application layers (Public for Load Balancers, Private App for EC2s, and Private DB for RDS).
Go to the Subnets section and create subnets with appropriate CIDR blocks: Public Subnets: Public-Subnet-A (10.0.1.0/24), Public-Subnet-B (10.0.2.0/24). Private App Subnets: Private-App-A (10.0.11.0/24), Private-App-B (10.0.12.0/24). Private DB Subnets: Private-DB-A (10.0.21.0/24), Private-DB-B (10.0.22.0/24). Create a public Route Table named Public-RT and associate it with the VPC. Under the Edit routes section of Public-RT, add a route with Destination 0.0.0.0/0 pointing Target to the Internet Gateway (igw-...). Associate Public-RT with two subnets: Public-Subnet-A and Public-Subnet-B. Create additional private Route Tables named Private-App-RT, ResumeMatching-Private-App-RT-B, and Private-DB-RT. Associate them with the App Subnets and DB Subnets respectively to ensure these layers are not exposed to the internet. 5.2.3. Configure NAT Gateway for Private Subnets Allows instances in the Private Subnets to make one-way outbound connections to the internet (to download packages and update software) while blocking any inbound connections initiated from the internet.
Allocate two static Elastic IPs named ResumeMatching-EIP and ResumeMatching-EIP-B. Create a NAT Gateway named ResumeMatching-NAT in Public-Subnet-A and associate it with the first Elastic IP. Create another NAT Gateway named ResumeMatching-NAT-B in the other Availability Zone and associate it with the second Elastic IP to ensure High Availability. Open the configurations of the Private App Route Tables, add a route for 0.0.0.0/0 and point the Target to the respective NAT Gateways.

title: Proposal
content:
AI Resume Matching & Interview Preparation Platform 1. Project Overview
Goal: A Web application that evaluates the matching degree between Resumes (CVs) and Job Descriptions (JDs), deployed on a standard AWS 3-Tier architecture. AI Processing: An EC2 cluster (Backend + Worker) is responsible for analyzing CVs and JDs by invoking OpenAI APIs via the Internet to optimize token consumption and minimize costs. 2. Cloud Infrastructure Architecture (AWS)
Edge & Global Layer:
Content Delivery Network (CloudFront), WAF firewall, and authentication service are currently planned but not deployed (AWS account verification required), meaning they are not active production services yet. Amazon S3 (Amazon Simple Storage Service) is used for general storage. Users interact via the Internet Gateway (1) or upload files directly via Pre-signed URLs to a dedicated S3 storage (S3 CV/JD Storage) (5). Networking & Load Balancing (Inbound):
VPC 10.0.0.0/16 serves as the foundation. Internet Gateway (IGW) acts as the single entry point connecting to the Internet for both Inbound and Outbound traffic (via NAT Gateway). An Application Load Balancer (ALB) is positioned across Availability Zones, receiving traffic from the IGW and distributing it to the Auto Scaling Group. Application Tier (Compute):
The EC2 cluster (Backend + Worker) is placed within isolated Private App Subnets (Private App Subnet A & Private App Subnet B), spanning across 2 Availability Zones (AZ A & AZ B) and managed by an Auto Scaling Group (ASG) to auto-scale based on load. Internal Communication: EC2 (AZ A) connects to the S3 storage service and SQS (6), backed by a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ). OpenAI API flow (AZ B) is executed by EC2 (AZ B) routing outbound traffic through the NAT Gateway of Public Subnet B to reach the Internet Gateway. (Note: NAT Gateways are deployed in both Public Subnet A and Public Subnet B to ensure routing optimization). Outbound & Internal Networking:
There are two NAT Gateways, one in Public Subnet A and one in Public Subnet B, ensuring High Availability for Outbound traffic originating from the Private Subnets. Communication to SQS and S3 is handled directly over the network, without utilizing VPC Endpoints. Database Tier:
Relational Database Service (RDS) running PostgreSQL configured with Multi-AZ. Two Private DB Subnets (Private DB Subnet A & Private DB Subnet B) are secured under a Security Group. RDS Primary (Master) is situated in AZ A. RDS Standby Replica is located in AZ B with real-time data replication (dashed line). Connection flow: EC2 in AZ A connects directly to the RDS Primary (7). EC2 in AZ B connects to the RDS Standby Replica via RDS Endpoints. Management & Monitoring:
A dedicated Management & Security zone (Regional Security & Management) hosts AWS Systems Manager, Amazon CloudWatch (logs), and AWS Secrets Manager (API Key). Deployment:
Source code is hosted on GitHub (Manual Deployment), designated as a manual deployment workflow. No automated AWS CodeBuild or AWS CodePipeline is used in the current architecture.

title: Worklog Week 2
content:
Objectives of Week 2: Master foundational concepts of VPC, Subnets, Security Groups, and EC2. Practice building basic network infrastructure and launching EC2 virtual servers. Understand AWS resource permission delegation (IAM Role for EC2) and monitor service costs. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Study concepts of VPC, Public/Private Subnets, Internet Gateway (IGW), and Route Tables to establish an isolated network environment. 27/04/2026 27/04/2026 Documentation Tue - Launch an Amazon EC2 instance.
- Configure Security Groups to allow SSH (Port 22) and HTTP (Port 80) traffic. 28/04/2026 28/04/2026 Reference Wed - Learn about IAM Roles and how they differ from IAM Users.
- Create an IAM Role for S3 Read-Only access and attach it to the EC2 instance. 29/04/2026 29/04/2026 Documentation Thu - Connect to the EC2 instance via SSH using a Key Pair from the local machine.
- Install Nginx/Apache web server on EC2 to serve a basic webpage. 30/04/2026 30/04/2026 Reference Fri - Check billing details for EC2 on the Billing Console.
- Complete the 3 foundational labs outlined in the online learning path. 01/05/2026 01/05/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 2: Understood VPC virtual network structure and successfully launched EC2 instances. Implemented secure IAM Roles for EC2 permissions (preventing Access Keys storage on servers). Successfully completed 3 foundational labs and accessed the Nginx web server from the Internet.

title: Event 2
content:
Harvest Report: “FCAJ Community Day (23/05/2026)” Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Day Event Date: Weekend morning (May 23, 2026) Location: 36th Floor, Bitexco Financial Tower, No. 02 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City (In-Person Event) Organizers: AWS Study Group in collaboration with the FCAJ Community Event Objectives Program Goal: Not just to listen to seminars, but to serve as a hub to connect, network, and inspire one another (with the chance of meeting potential partners or like-minded peers). Key Messages: Centered around topics spanning AI, Cloud, and real-world stories drawn from the experiences of industry experts. Discussions included Prompt Optimization, AWS technologies (like CloudFront), the Hackathon journey, and the mindset for building business-grade AI architecture (Enterprise grade). Value for Attendees: Help attendees understand job market dynamics under the impact of AI, how to build core foundations to prevent obsolescence, and equip them with problem-solving mindsets for real-world enterprise challenges. Speaker List Tinh Truong – Platform Engineer at GoTymeX Thinh Nguyen – DevOps Engineer | FCAJ Ambassador Anh Pham – Cloud Consultant at G-AsiaPacific Vietnam Uyen Le – GenAI Engineer at VIB Thao Nguyen – GenAI Engineer at VIB Mai Nguyen – GenAI Engineer at VIB Duc Dao – Solutions Architect at Cloud Kinetics Vy Lam – Senior Business Systems Analyst at VPBank Event Highlights Problem Overview: AI is reducing the cost of software development, which will cause a massive surge in software demand. This will create many new jobs in operations (Platform engineering) and debugging (fixing AI-generated code). Another issue is that generating code using AI without proper context produces bloated, low-quality outputs (LLM hallucinations). Solutions Introduced: Set clear system prompts and context (Context/System Prompt) including: Goal, Persona, and Format instead of asking vague questions. Use a Multi-Agent System (orchestration of multiple agents) to solve complex tasks (e.g., credit analysis for Startups) rather than stuffing everything into a single AI model. Technologies, Services, & Tools: AWS: CloudFront (Flat-rate pricing, content compression, DDoS protection), AWS Bedrock, EC2, Lambda, VPC. AI/LLM: Amazon Agent platform, Obsidian (for building a Second Brain), MCP (Model Context Protocol). Other: Terraform (Infrastructure as Code - IaC). Demos & Case Studies: UTM Morpho Project: Demoed a hackathon tool that uses 3 AI agents to generate HTML/UI from images, allowing users to drag and drop or edit layouts directly on the interface. LLM Parameters Demo: Ran comparisons with Temperature = 0 on AWS Bedrock vs. Local to show that cloud-based AI outputs still display variance due to inference optimization mechanisms. Key Takeaways: In enterprise environments, security compliance and risk assessment (Guardrails, Security, Audit) are critical—far more important than simply connecting numerous AI tools. Key Learnings Mindset & Methodology: LLM is a “Probabilistic engine” (probability-based model), so do not assume it will always return the exact same output even when setting Temperature = 0. Do not let AI go on complete auto-pilot; always keep humans in the loop (Human in the loop). Technical Knowledge: Understand how CloudFront optimizes costs via flat-rate pricing to prevent “billing shock” caused by high traffic spikes or DDoS attacks. Best Practices: Avoid the “Internet puller” syndrome (blindly copying libraries or code snippets from the internet without deep comprehension of the context). Real-world Insights: In a Hackathon, having too many ideas (over-generation) wastes time. Limit the scope and focus on solving one core problem (such as enhancing the editing experience) to complete the prototype within the 36-hour limit. Application to My Work Current Project Applicability: Design AI systems based on Multi-agent principles: assign specific tasks to specialized agents (e.g., Financial Analysis Agent, Market Research Agent). Implement mTLS and VPC Origin to secure resources fully. Technologies to Explore: Use Terraform to deploy infrastructure as code (IaC) for easy version management. Test edge protection features of AWS CloudFront (geo-blocking, automatic bot blocking). Workflow Improvement Ideas: Instead of downloading data manually, integrate AI Agents with tools (such as using MCP to connect Excel) to automate dashboard creation or meeting summaries. Event Experience Speaker Insights: Realized that in professional environments, employers do not just ask for demos but probe deeply into the practicality of the “Product” and its business use cases. Hands-on Experience: Witnessed a live demo of a product built in 36 hours and saw a visual illustration comparing LLM behavior locally vs. on the cloud. Networking: Encouraged self-confidence and public speaking, highlighting the fierce competition in the job market. Most Memorable Aspect: The strong warning about directly pasting ChatGPT-generated code into enterprise systems, which can degrade coding standards and introduce severe security holes. Key Takeaways Most Important Concept: Core foundations (Foundation), university degrees, and basic software engineering skills are still mandatory. AI is a bonus, not a replacement for core software engineering principles (such as secure password encoding/decoding). Real-world Experience: To convince executives to approve budgets for AI projects, you must clearly demonstrate ROI and concrete financial savings—“Numbers speak louder than words”. Future Development Plan: Dive deeper into AI Mindset and AI Adoption. Keep in mind the system design rule: It must not only run, but “run securely, run reliably, and serve the user”. Read official documentation carefully when configuring AI parameters instead of relying on online chatter. Selected Event Photos Speaker sharing about Context and System Prompt
Agenda and program introduction
Presenting from Zero to Idea Hackathon journey

title: Blog 3
content:
A Very Cool Feature of AWS Lake Formation Recently while researching Data Lakes on AWS, I came across a very interesting article from AWS about Lake Formation that I wanted to share with everyone.
Looking at the diagram, we can see:
EMR Spark reads/writes data directly from/to S3 via IAM Permissions. Lake Formation manages permissions on database tables in the Glue Catalog. Athena queries data based on Lake Formation permissions. This leads to a very common scenario: You can query data using Athena normally, but when using Spark to read files directly in S3, you get an Access Denied error.
This happens because Athena and Spark use two different authorization mechanisms:
Athena → Lake Formation Spark reading S3 files → IAM / S3 Policy The New AWS Solution AWS recently introduced a new feature to solve this issue. Lake Formation can now grant temporary credentials to Spark for direct S3 access based on permissions configured in Lake Formation, via the API: GetTemporaryDataLocationCredentials()
Data Access Flow Diagram Key Benefits of This Feature: Reduced overhead of managing permissions in multiple places: Unifies the authorization mechanism under a single management plane. Fewer Access Denied errors due to misconfiguration: Syncs access permissions between direct S3 file accesses and queries. More convenient for Spark, ETL, and Machine Learning workloads: Integrates big data pipeline engines more seamlessly. Easier monitoring of data access via CloudTrail: Audits access logs centrally for audit compliance. Some Considerations: The S3 Location must be registered with Lake Formation. Requires Amazon EMR 6.15.0+ or 7.1.0+. Apache Iceberg is not yet supported. Cross-Region access is not yet supported. I find this a very useful improvement for Data Lake architectures on AWS, especially in environments utilizing both Athena and EMR Spark.
Article Link: https://www.facebook.com/groups/awsstudygroupfcj/posts/2191055074992786/?notif_id=1782041739635661&notif_t=group_post_approved&ref=notif
Reference Guide: https://aws.amazon.com/vi/blogs/big-data/access-amazon-s3-data-files-directly-using-aws-lake-formation-permissions/?fbclid=IwY2xjawSu8LtleHRuA2FlbQIxMABicmlkETFDd1poRWJtcTNFMEw4bmJKc3J0YwZhcHBfaWQQMjIyMDM5MTc4ODIwMDg5MgABHlfRqurG5Rir4FRt1bIoRDfriCsPR9wuIOYoTnZdypKxZVcIBVgtWsk4A8C9_aem_pW5Px8mN6tGNyQOA7DJaXA

title: Security & Access Management
content:
5.3. Security and Access Management 5.3.1. Initialize Security Groups Configure virtual firewalls to control network traffic between resources at the protocol and port level.
ALB Security Group (ResumeMatching-ALB-SG): Open HTTP (80) and HTTPS (443) ports (TCP) with Source 0.0.0.0/0 to allow web access. Backend Security Group (ResumeMatching-Backend-SG): Configure Inbound rules to allow HTTP protocol on TCP. The Source is restricted to traffic originating only from ResumeMatching-ALB-SG. Worker Security Group (ResumeMatching-Worker-SG): Virtual firewall safeguarding the background Worker Server. Database Security Group (ResumeMatching-RDS-SG): Open port 5432 (PostgreSQL). The Source strictly allows internal connections only from the Backend and Worker Security Groups. 5.3.2. Configure IAM Roles Provide secure authorization for EC2 instances to communicate with other AWS services without hard-coding static Access Keys.
Navigate to the IAM service, create a role named ResumeMatching-Backend-Role to associate with the backend EC2 server. Create a second role named ResumeMatching-Worker-Role dedicated to the Worker server. Attach AWS Managed policies like AmazonSQSFullAccess and appropriate S3 manipulation policies.

title: Worklog Week 3
content:
Objectives of Week 3: Master object storage using Amazon S3 and secure data using Bucket Policies. Deploy relational database RDS MySQL and NoSQL database DynamoDB. Set up AWS Cloud9 development environment and utilize AWS CLI to manage resources. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Create an Amazon S3 Bucket.
- Configure Static Website Hosting to deploy static web interface.
- Create S3 Bucket Policies to control public/VPC-only access. 04/05/2026 04/05/2026 Documentation Tue - Provision an RDS MySQL database in a secure Private Subnet.
- Establish secure database connectivity from EC2 using Security Group rules. 05/05/2026 05/05/2026 Reference Wed - Create a NoSQL table in DynamoDB.
- Write scripts to perform basic CRUD operations on DynamoDB. 06/05/2026 06/05/2026 Documentation Thu - Provision an integrated development environment (IDE) using AWS Cloud9. 07/05/2026 07/05/2026 Reference Fri - Install and configure AWS CLI on Cloud9.
- Write shell scripts using AWS CLI commands to automate creating, listing, and cleaning resources. 08/05/2026 08/05/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 3: Successfully deployed a static website on S3 and configured secure Bucket Policies. Deployed an RDS MySQL database in Private Subnet with secure access allowed from EC2. Created a DynamoDB table and performed data queries via CLI. Mastered basic AWS CLI command automation and cloud-based development using AWS Cloud9.

title: Event 3
content:
Harvest Report: “First Cloud Journey Community Day (06/06/2026)” Event Information Event Name: First Cloud Journey (FCJ) Community Day Event Date: June 6, 2026 Location: 36th Floor, Bitexco Financial Tower, No. 02 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City Organizers: Team AWS G3 in collaboration with the First Cloud Journey Community Event Objectives Program Goal: To share practical knowledge on career roadmaps to become Cloud, Security, and DevOps Engineers; update participants on new technologies like Machine Learning (ML), Docker, and AI (GraphRAG). Value for Attendees: Foundations to advanced concepts about cloud infrastructure, gaining real-world experience from speakers currently working at large enterprises. Event Highlights Network Security with ML: Introduced a Machine Learning-based Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS) integrated with AWS WAF to overcome traditional ruleset limitations. The solution uses the CSE-CIC-IDS2018 dataset for real-world model training. Containerization: Introduction to Docker, comparing VMs and Containers, highlighting Docker’s lightweight nature and its “Build once, run anywhere” capability. Career Roadmap: Shared a personal journey from IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin, emphasizing that Linux and Networking are essential foundations. Multiplayer in the Cloud: Used AWS WebSockets, Lambda, and DynamoDB to connect Godot game clients for real-time data communication. Artificial Intelligence (AI): Built a GraphRAG application using Amazon Bedrock and Amazon Neptune to optimize LLM information retrieval through graph-based relationships. Soft Skills: “The Art of Effective Teamwork” with 4 golden rules and tools like Trello and Slack. Key Learnings Mindset & Methodology: Cloud is not just a technology but a new way of thinking (Cloud Mindset) featuring automation, elastic scaling, and pay-as-you-go pricing models. Technical Knowledge: Methods to handle imbalanced data in ML, basic Docker CLI commands, and real-time WebSockets architecture on AWS. Best Practices: Never test on production environments to maintain system availability and business credibility. Automate repetitive tasks and maintain thorough documentation. Data quality is vital for ML model performance. Application to My Work Current Project: Utilize Docker to standardize development and deployment environments (CI/CD). Deploy AWS WAF to protect web applications against vulnerabilities like SQL Injection or XSS. Technologies to Explore: Adopt Amazon Bedrock for GenAI features or AWS GameLift for managing dedicated game servers. Workflow Improvement: Build a real-time monitoring dashboard to enhance threat visibility. Event Experience Practice & Demos: Attended live demos showing the NIDS system, Docker CLI operations, and real-time Godot game client synchronization via AWS. Networking: Connected with speakers via LinkedIn QR codes to exchange professional guidance. Most Memorable Aspect: The powerful synergy between AWS cloud services and modern technologies like ML/AI to solve practical security and operational challenges. Key Takeaways Most Critical Knowledge: Signature-based security is insufficient against zero-day exploits; ML-driven security is necessary. Real-world Experience: Starting point does not matter; consistency and hands-on practice on real projects matter more than just collecting certifications. Future Development Plan: Focus deeply on 1-2 core skills before expanding, building a personal portfolio with practical projects. Selected Event Photos Introduction to Docker Containerization technology
Career roadmap from IT Helpdesk to Senior Sysadmin
Building GraphRAG applications with Bedrock and Neptune
Multiplayer in the Cloud with WebSockets and Godot

title: Blogs Posted
content:
This section lists and introduces the blogs I have posted to AWS Study Group:
Blog 1 - SYSTEM MONITORING ON AWS: STREAMING CLOUDWATCH METRICS TO OPENTELEMETRY COLLECTOR IN VPC USING LAMBDA This blog introduces a push-based observability solution using AWS Lambda as a secure relay station to stream metrics from CloudWatch Metric Streams directly to an internal OpenTelemetry Collector isolated inside a private VPC subnet.
Blog 2 - BUILDING AN AI GATEWAY TO GOVERN AMAZON BEDROCK WITH API GATEWAY This blog shares practical design experience building an AI Gateway using Amazon API Gateway, Lambda Authorizers, and Lambda Integrations to authorize client calls, manage tenant isolation, rate limit requests, and govern large language model (LLM) costs on Amazon Bedrock.
Blog 3 - A VERY COOL FEATURE OF AWS LAKE FORMATION This blog discusses the new capability in AWS Lake Formation to issue temporary credentials via the GetTemporaryDataLocationCredentials() API, allowing big data engines like EMR Spark to directly read and write files in S3 based on unified Lake Formation permissions.

title: Storage & Queue Services
content:
5.4. Storage and Queue Services 5.4.1. Initialize S3 Bucket Switch to the S3 service, and click Create bucket to create storage space for candidate CVs and documents. Define a bucket name following the pattern resume-matching-storage-[AWS-ACCOUNT-ID]-us-east-1. Under the Permissions tab, modify the Cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) configuration. Add the JSON structure specifying AllowedMethods to include GET, PUT, and POST to enable the web application to upload files directly to the bucket. 5.4.2. Initialize SQS Queues Utilize message queuing to decouple heavy AI processing workloads from the main web server response cycle.
Navigate to the SQS interface. Create a Standard queue named ResumeMatching-DLQ (Dead Letter Queue) to catch messages that fail during processing. Initialize the main queue named ResumeMatching-Queue. Enable the Dead-letter queue feature on the main queue, point it to the ARN of ResumeMatching-DLQ, and set Maximum receives to 3.

title: Event 4
content:
Harvest Report: “Study Tour: AWS — Enterprise Cloud Architectures & Industry Applications” Event Information Event Name: Study Tour: AWS — Enterprise Cloud Architectures & Industry Applications Event Date: Saturday, July 4, 2026 Location: Bitexco Financial Tower, No. 02 Hai Trieu Street, Saigon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City Organizers: Swinburne Vietnam in collaboration with AWS Vietnam, Renova Cloud, and Cloud Kinetics Event Objectives Program Goal: To provide students with hands-on experience in Cloud Computing and Generative AI (GenAI) in enterprises, allowing them to connect and learn directly from top industry experts for career orientation. Key Messages: Outlining the recruitment landscape in Cloud, the roadmap to transition from a student into a real Data/Cloud Engineer, how enterprises optimize data architectures and apply GenAI in the real world, and a Fireside Chat inspiring the next generation of tech talent. Value for Attendees: Exploring the big picture of Cloud/AI markets, bridging the gap between classroom theory and real-world execution to stand out to employers, and cultivating a problem-solving mindset for business challenges. Speakers / Experts Top experts from AWS Vietnam Solutions Architects and Engineers from Renova Cloud Experts from Cloud Kinetics and outstanding GenAI engineers Event Highlights Job Market & Career Trends: In-depth analysis of the shift in global Cloud Computing recruitment, particularly the trend of integrating AI/ML into Cloud infrastructure. Highlighting emerging roles like Cloud FinOps, AI/ML Cloud Architect, and Data Engineer, offering fresh graduates golden opportunities for competitive advantages. From Theory to Execution - Data Architecture: Real-world stories of transitioning from an IT student to a professional Data Engineer and Cloud Engineer. Concrete Case Studies on how large enterprises optimize their Data Architectures to save costs, reduce latency, and improve Big Data processing performance. Fireside Chat - Shaping the Next Generation of Talent: An inspiring discussion between AWS experts and tech leaders on methods to nurture, mentor, and develop the next generation of talented Cloud engineers/architects in Vietnam. Discussions on building a culture of innovation and a mindset of continuous self-learning in a fast-paced technology environment. 💡 Key Takeaway: While technologies and tools change every single day, a solid problem-solving mindset, flexible adaptability, and a proactive “hands-on” attitude remain the ultimate golden keys to staying ahead of the curve. Key Learnings Hands-on Mindset: Be proactive in turning classroom theory into workable solutions. Focus on solving business problems rather than just learning tool features. Value of Networking: Connecting directly with experts and speakers provides highly valuable and realistic career guidance. Application to My Work / Studies Focus on building personal/group projects that solve real-world problems (e.g., optimizing data architecture, integrating GenAI). Proactively learn and practice AWS labs to gain hands-on experience in preparation for job applications. Selected Event Photos Group photo at AWS office
Speaker discussing Job Market & Career Trends
Speaker presenting on Enterprise Cloud Architectures
Sharing skills and mindset in the AI era

title: Worklog Week 4
content:
Objectives of Week 4: Configure Auto Scaling Groups (ASG) and Application Load Balancer (ALB) for high availability. Monitor resources, gather logs, and set up system alarms using Amazon CloudWatch. Manage DNS resolution using Route 53 and optimize network-level security (NACLs/Security Groups). Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Create Launch Templates, configure Auto Scaling Groups (ASG) and Application Load Balancers (ALB) for load distribution. 11/05/2026 11/05/2026 Documentation Tue - Test Auto Scaling behavior by running a CPU stress script on active EC2 instances. 12/05/2026 12/05/2026 Reference Wed - Configure CloudWatch Metrics and create CloudWatch Alarms to send email alerts via SNS when CPU exceeds 80%. 13/05/2026 13/05/2026 Documentation Thu - Collect application logs using the CloudWatch Logs Agent to track software exceptions. 14/05/2026 14/05/2026 Reference Fri - Purchase/Configure a domain name in Route 53, pointing its DNS alias to the ALB.
- Set up optimized NACLs and Security Groups to secure traffic flow. 15/05/2026 15/05/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 4: Successfully configured ASG and ALB, enabling the application infrastructure to handle sudden traffic surges. Set up CloudWatch Alarms that automatically trigger email alerts upon anomalies. Pointed Route 53 domain alias to the ALB and enhanced security using customized network rules.

title: AWS Workshop
content:
Workshop: Deploying AI Resume Matching & Interview Preparation Platform on AWS This lab provides detailed instructions on how to comprehensively deploy the Resume Fit system on the AWS cloud platform. Resume Fit is an AI application that helps quickly and transparently evaluate the compatibility between candidate profiles (CVs) and job descriptions (JDs) to support recruitment decisions.
The system is designed following standard architecture on AWS, ensuring high scalability and availability.

title: Relational Database (RDS)
content: 5.5. Relational Database Service (RDS) Setup 5.5.1. Configure Secrets Manager Navigate to AWS Secrets Manager, and create a secret named ResumeMatching-Secrets to secure sensitive values. Configure the Secret value containing key/value entries such as HOST (to be updated later with the RDS Endpoint), PORT (5432), USERNAME, PASSWORD, and the application’s OPENAI_API_KEY. 5.5.2. Create RDS PostgreSQL Database Open the RDS console, select Subnet groups and create a database subnet group named resumematching-dbsubnetgroup referencing the project VPC, grouping subnets Private-DB-A and Private-DB-B together. Proceed to initialize the Database (DB identifier: resume-matching-db) with the PostgreSQL Engine and instance class db.t3.micro. Once successfully created, copy the Database Endpoint and update the HOST key value in Secrets Manager.

title: Worklog Week 5
content:
Objectives of Week 5: Implement Serverless architecture using AWS Lambda and Amazon API Gateway. Research Artificial Intelligence (AI) integration by connecting and invoking Amazon Bedrock models. Practice secure sensitive information management using AWS Secrets Manager. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Write AWS Lambda serverless functions using Python/Node.js to run backend logic. 18/05/2026 18/05/2026 Documentation Tue - Setup REST/HTTP APIs using API Gateway to serve as endpoints for Lambda functions. 19/05/2026 19/05/2026 Reference Wed - Handle CORS and configure security on API Gateway endpoints using IAM Authorizers or Cognito. 20/05/2026 20/05/2026 Documentation Thu - Research Amazon Bedrock and request model access (Claude, Llama) on the console.
- Code Lambda to invoke Bedrock APIs. 21/05/2026 21/05/2026 Reference Fri - Use AWS Secrets Manager to store database credentials and external API keys securely instead of hardcoding them. 22/05/2026 22/05/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 5: Successfully established a Serverless architecture flow (API Gateway -> Lambda). Integrated Amazon Bedrock to invoke LLM text generation directly from Lambda backend. Secured database connection credentials and api keys using AWS Secrets Manager.

title: Compute & Server Configuration
content: 5.6. Compute Deployment and Server Configuration 5.6.1. Launch EC2 Backend Instance Create an RSA Key Pair named ResumeMatching-Key. In the EC2 Dashboard, launch an Instance named ResumeMatching-Backend using instance type t3.micro placed in the application VPC. Wait for the Instance state to transition to Running and the Status checks to display 2/2 checks passed. 5.6.2. Connect and Install Software Securely connect to the EC2 instance using Systems Manager Fleet Manager. Choose the running Managed node and click Start terminal session. Execute the Git installation command to fetch the source code: sudo dnf install git Install and start Docker to run containerized application: sudo dnf install -y docker sudo systemctl enable docker sudo systemctl start docker Navigate to the /opt folder and clone the source code from GitHub: cd /opt sudo git clone http://github.com/thuanthien-tran/resume-fit.git sudo chown -R ssm-user /opt/resume-fit

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my 12-week internship at the First Cloud Journey (FCJ) community from April 20, 2026 to July 10, 2026, I had the valuable opportunity to grow, challenge myself, and apply classroom theory to a professional environment.
Serving as the Team Leader for the final project “AI Resume Matching & Interview Preparation Platform”, I was not only directly involved in designing the 3-Tier system architecture on AWS and coding AI features, but also responsible for coordinating tasks, managing timelines, and fostering collaboration within the team. This hands-on leadership experience significantly elevated both my specialized technical capabilities (AWS Cloud, Generative AI, DevOps) and my team management skills.
Based on my dedicated performance and the actual project outcomes achieved, I evaluate my performance during the internship at a Good-to-Excellent level based on the following criteria:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of the field, applying knowledge in practice, proficiency with tools, work quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to absorb new knowledge and learn quickly ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Taking initiative, seeking out tasks without waiting for instructions ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing tasks on time and ensuring quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, rules, and work processes ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Willingness to receive feedback and improve oneself ✅ ☐ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas clearly, coordinating team members, and reporting progress ✅ ☐ ☐ 8 Teamwork Working effectively with colleagues and leading the team to success ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Respecting colleagues, partners, and the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Identifying problems, proposing solutions, and showing creativity ✅ ☐ ☐ 11 Contribution to project/team Work effectiveness, innovative ideas, and overall leadership contribution ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ✅ ☐ ☐ Areas for Improvement (A Leader’s Perspective) While I successfully achieved our technical and project management goals, I recognize key areas where I can continue to grow to achieve higher professional standards:
Optimizing Task Delegation: During fast-paced project phases, I occasionally took on too many complex technical tasks myself rather than breaking them down and delegating them to team members. I need to improve delegation to empower my teammates and free up time for strategic oversight. Time Management Under Pressure: Balancing deep technical research (such as optimizing token consumption for OpenAI APIs and configuring EC2 Worker instances) with managing the team’s daily tasks was challenging. This sometimes resulted in personal worklogs being deferred to weekends. I plan to cultivate better discipline in daily time allocation. Deepening Cloud Architecture Knowledge: I aim to explore advanced AWS security configurations (such as mTLS and VPC Origin, Amazon Bedrock Guardrails) and leverage Terraform to standardize our entire cloud infrastructure as code (IaC) in a more professional manner.

title: Worklog Week 6
content:
Objectives of Week 6: Protect web applications from common web attacks and DDoS using AWS WAF and AWS Shield. Understand DevOps tools such as AWS CodeCommit and CodePipeline for automation. Review and synthesize knowledge from 20+ completed practice labs and evaluate team capabilities. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Create Web ACLs in AWS WAF and define rules against SQL Injection, Cross-Site Scripting (XSS), and Rate Limiting. 25/05/2026 25/05/2026 Documentation Tue - Associate Web ACLs with the ALB or API Gateway. Perform security checks by sending malicious mock payloads. 26/05/2026 26/05/2026 Reference Wed - Set up repos on AWS CodeCommit or link GitHub.
- Learn CodeBuild, CodeDeploy, and CodePipeline to automate software builds and deployment. 27/05/2026 27/05/2026 Documentation Thu - Review and summarize all completed 20+ AWS hands-on labs in the FCAJ syllabus. 28/05/2026 28/05/2026 Reference Fri - Hold a team meeting to assess capabilities and divide roles for the project: Team Lead (Infra/Sec), Dev (Code), Tester (QA/CloudWatch). 29/05/2026 29/05/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 6: Deployed Web ACLs to successfully secure the public endpoints from bad requests. Gained fundamental understanding of DevOps CI/CD pipeline automation on AWS. Finished auditing 20+ labs and agreed on project team responsibilities.

title: Auto Scaling & Load Balancing
content: 5.7. Auto Scaling and Load Balancing Configuration 5.7.1. Configure AMI and Launch Template Standardize the configured EC2 environment by creating an Amazon Machine Image (AMI) named ResumeMatching-Backend-AMI. From this AMI, create a Launch Template named ResumeMatching-LT containing standard hardware, networking, and security configurations. 5.7.2. Auto Scaling Group (ASG) Create an Auto Scaling Group named ResumeMatching-ASG and link it to the ResumeMatching-LT Launch Template. Edit the Desired capacity to 2. The ASG will automatically launch new EC2 instances in an Initializing state for redundancy and scaling. 5.7.3. Target Group and Application Load Balancer Create a Target Group named ResumeMatching-TG with target type Instance using HTTP protocol on port 8080 (or 80 depending on container config). Initialize an Application Load Balancer (ALB) named ResumeMatching-ALB. Select the Scheme as Internet-facing to receive traffic from the internet, listen across 2 Availability Zones, and route traffic to the backend instances via the Target Group.

title: Sharing & Feedback
content:
General Evaluation of the First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) Program Having completed the 12-week First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) program, I would like to share my honest reflections and feedback regarding my learning and working experience:
1. Learning & Working Environment
The FCAJ community environment is highly dynamic, open, and strongly encourages self-learning. Although it is a hybrid internship program combining online learning with offline events (Community Days and Study Tours), I always felt a strong sense of connection and a professional, enterprise-like working atmosphere.
2. Mentorship & Admin Support
Mentors and Admins provided exceptional and timely support, especially when students encountered AWS infrastructure or account issues. The mentors focused on guiding our troubleshooting mindsets and problem-solving methodologies rather than just handing out answers, which greatly improved my debugging capabilities.
3. Alignment Between Work & Academic Major
The curriculum—ranging from basic networking labs and IAM security configurations to serverless backend development (API Gateway, Lambda) and AI integration (Amazon Bedrock)—perfectly aligned with and supported my Software Engineering / Information Systems major. The final project “AI Resume Matching & Interview Preparation Platform” successfully turned theoretical 3-tier architecture concepts into a working application.
4. Learning & Skill Development Opportunities
The program equipped me with a solid cloud foundation and practical hands-on experience. More importantly, serving as the Team Leader allowed me to practice project planning, task management via Trello, hosting progress review meetings, and coordinating code reviews.
5. Culture & Teamwork
The sharing and mutual support culture is the brightest highlight of FCAJ. During our group project development, team members willingly stayed up late to debug integration issues between the Front-end and Back-end. This strong collaborative spirit was a major driving force for me as a leader.
6. Community Events
Events such as the FCAJ Community Day and the Study Tour at AWS Office (26th/36th floors of Bitexco Tower) were fantastic opportunities to network and learn directly from leading Solutions Architects at AWS Vietnam, helping bridge the gap between academia and industry.
Response to Survey Questions What did you find most satisfying during the internship?
The practical nature of the curriculum and the opportunity to own a complete AI-Cloud project from architectural design to deployment. Additionally, collaborating and learning alongside talented peers from other universities (such as HUTECH and Swinburne) provided highly valuable relationships and insights. What areas do you think the program should improve for future cohorts?
The program could introduce basic guides or workshops on Terraform for Infrastructure as Code (IaC) in the mid-weeks. This would help final project teams manage and deploy their cloud resources consistently with fewer manual configuration errors. Would you recommend this program to your peers? Why?
Definitely YES. It is an excellent launching pad for any student wanting to pursue Cloud, DevOps, or AI career paths. The program does not just teach how to use tools; it teaches how to solve real business problems—which is exactly what employers look for. Suggestions & Future Outlook Experience Improvement Suggestion: The organizing committee could set up a centralized troubleshooting channel (e.g., Discord or a forum categorized by Lab weeks) so students can search for and resolve common lab errors more quickly. Future Involvement: I would love to remain involved in the First Cloud Journey community as a sub-mentor or event support volunteer to help guide future cohorts and continue promoting cloud learning.

title: Worklog Week 7
content: Objectives of Week 7: Design the overall High-Level Design (HLD) architecture diagram for the capstone project. Establish the official project virtual network (VPC) with secure partitioned subnets. Set up granular IAM roles and group policies for team members based on project tasks. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Brainstorm ideas for the capstone project: AI-Powered Customer Support Application. 01/06/2026 01/06/2026 Documentation Tue - Design the system’s High-Level Design (HLD) architecture diagram using modeling software (e.g., Draw.io). 02/06/2026 02/06/2026 Reference Wed - Plan Database entity relationships (ERD) and backend integration workflows. 03/06/2026 03/06/2026 Documentation Thu - Deploy the production VPC network layout: 2 Public and 2 Private Subnets stretched across 2 Availability Zones for High Availability. 04/06/2026 04/06/2026 Reference Fri - Create IAM Groups, Users, and Roles to allocate development privileges according to the principle of least privilege. 05/06/2026 05/06/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 7: Finished designing the project High-Level Design (HLD) diagram. Successfully deployed a Multi-AZ VPC network infrastructure layout for the project. Configured secure IAM policies for all team members.

title: System Monitoring
content:
5.8. System Monitoring and High Availability (Monitoring) Monitoring is mandatory to ensure traffic is correctly distributed and Auto Scaling rules trigger as expected.
5.8.1. Verify Auto Scaling Group Operations Access the EC2 service, and navigate to the Instances menu. Observe the system automatically creating new EC2 instances named ResumeMatching-ASG-Instance distributed evenly across different Availability Zones. Some older instances may display a Terminated status. This proves that the ASG is functioning correctly: automatically reclaiming old instances and launching new ones upon configuration updates or instance failure. 5.8.2. Verify Target Group Health Checks Under the Load Balancing section, select Target Groups and click on the system’s Target Group. Switch to the Targets tab and observe the Registered targets table. Confirm that the Health status column displays a green Healthy status for the instances, proving the web server has started and is ready to accept requests from the Load Balancer. 5.8.3. Performance Monitoring via CloudWatch Access CloudWatch and select the Metrics menu to monitor deeper parameters. For EC2: Filter for the EC2 Namespace, and choose CPUUtilization. Observe the graphs to spot usage spikes (e.g., 81.9%) when the AI executes heavy tasks, which helps you set scaling thresholds for the ASG. For ALB: Filter metrics for RequestCount and TargetResponseTime. The correlation between the spike in request count and the change in response time (e.g., 660.4 ms) helps evaluate the smoothness of the user experience.

title: Worklog Week 8
content:
Objectives of Week 8: Deploy web servers on EC2 with ALB load balancing and RDS/DynamoDB database persistence. Build Backend REST APIs using API Gateway and AWS Lambda for serverless computation. Realize intelligent features by calling Large Language Models on Amazon Bedrock from Lambda. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Set up EC2 instances hosting the Web Frontend and link them to the ALB. 08/06/2026 08/06/2026 Documentation Tue - Deploy Database engines (RDS or DynamoDB) in Private Subnets. Set up Security Group rules to limit connection access to EC2/Lambda. 09/06/2026 09/06/2026 Reference Wed - Program Backend APIs on AWS Lambda and expose them using API Gateway. 10/06/2026 10/06/2026 Documentation Thu - Assign IAM Role policies allowing Lambda to access DynamoDB and Amazon Bedrock.
- Code Lambda to invoke Claude models on Amazon Bedrock. 11/06/2026 11/06/2026 Reference Fri - Code Web Frontend (React/Static HTML) to consume API Gateway endpoints.
- Run End-to-End integration tests between Web, API, DB, and AI. 12/06/2026 12/06/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 8: Deployed front-facing EC2 web servers and isolated RDS/DynamoDB databases. Built a working serverless API flow integrated with Amazon Bedrock AI. Completed integration testing of the whole application flow.

title: Application Testing
content:
5.9. Testing the Resume Fit Application 5.9.1. Verify Backend and Database Connectivity via ALB Test using the ALB API endpoint (e.g., http://[ALB-DNS-Name]/api/ready) to receive the JSON response {"status": "ready"} proving the Backend and Database have successfully connected. 5.9.2. Step-by-Step UI Flow Walkthrough 1. Register & Login to System Access the Frontend Domain/IP. Create an account and log in via the Register and Login screens to enter your workspace.
User Registration UI
User Login UI
2. Job Description (JD) Management Create and manage the catalog of Job Descriptions (JDs) for profile matching.
List of Managed Job Descriptions
Step 1: Initialize Job Position Details
3. Upload CV & JD for Analysis Run the core workflow: upload candidate CVs (PDF/Docx format) mapping to the selected JD.
Step 2: Select Job Description and Upload Candidate CV
Review files before initiating system analysis
4. AI Resume Matching Reports Initiate the analysis. The AI backend (Worker) invokes the OpenAI API to yield a comprehensive Candidate Analysis Report:
Score Analysis: Overview of rank level compatibility and overall matching percentage (e.g., 60%). Skill Analysis: Highlights missing professional skills (e.g., Cybersecurity, C#, ELK, etc.). Recommendation & Interview Questions: Automatically generates technical interview questions tailored precisely to the candidate’s actual projects found in their CV. Step 3: Overview of CV evaluation results
Detailed score analysis charts
Candidate SWOT and detailed breakdown
Job role compatibility details
Matching skills vs. missing gaps
AI recommendation summary on candidate
Generated technical interview questions
Raw text extracted from candidate CV
5. Candidate Catalog, Ranking & Debugger The system logs matching history and ranks candidates based on their suitability to help HR make quick comparisons.
Manage CV database inventory
Chronological match history logs
Candidate matching rank leaderboard
System debugger and container logs console

title: Worklog Week 9
content: Objectives of Week 9: Perform Load Testing on the infrastructure to evaluate Auto Scaling Group scaling performance. Create centralized CloudWatch Dashboards and audit administrative actions using CloudTrail. Analyze project spending trends via AWS Cost Explorer and implement cost-saving optimizations. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Run load testing tools (like Apache Bench, Locust) against the ALB endpoint. 15/06/2026 15/06/2026 Documentation Tue - Observe Auto Scaling Group metrics to verify successful scale-up and scale-down behaviors. 16/06/2026 16/06/2026 Reference Wed - Build a centralized CloudWatch Dashboard displaying EC2 CPU metrics, ALB Request Counts, Lambda Latencies, and RDS Connections. 17/06/2026 17/06/2026 Documentation Thu - Enable AWS CloudTrail auditing. Run IAM Access Analyzer to scan for over-permissive policies. 18/06/2026 18/06/2026 Reference Fri - Analyze AWS Billing dashboards and Cost Explorer charts. Terminate unused volumes and scale down DB instances to optimize costs. 19/06/2026 19/06/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 9: The system passed stress testing and auto-scaled out under heavy simulation load. Completed a CloudWatch Dashboard for real-time monitoring of resource health. Shaved off 25% of infrastructure expenses through optimization cleanups.

title: Clean up Resources
content:
5.10. Clean up Resources (Clean up) After completing the workshop and testing, to avoid incurring unnecessary storage and infrastructure maintenance costs on AWS, proceed with cleaning up resources in the following order:
Delete the Auto Scaling Group to prevent the system from automatically relaunching servers, then manually Terminate any remaining EC2 Instances. Delete the Application Load Balancer and Target Group. Delete the RDS Database (uncheck final Snapshot creation if data retention is unnecessary) and the RDS Subnet Group. Delete the NAT Gateways, wait for the status to transition to Deleted, then Release the Elastic IPs. Tear down VPC Endpoints, Route Tables, Internet Gateways, Subnets, and finally delete the VPC itself. Empty all objects within the S3 bucket, then proceed to Delete the Bucket. Delete Secrets Manager secrets. Delete SQS Queues. Delete Key Pairs and IAM Roles created for the lab.

title: Worklog Week 10
content: Objectives of Week 10: Finalize UI styling and functional bugs, freezing the codebase and infrastructure configuration. Execute a 24-hour continuous system test to evaluate deployment reliability. Create presentation slides, record product demo video, and compile the final technical report. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date References Mon - Fix remaining UI glitches and backend bugs. Freeze codebase on Git. 22/06/2026 22/06/2026 Documentation Tue - Do final reviews of firewall settings (Security Groups, AWS WAF rules) before deployment audits. 23/06/2026 23/06/2026 Reference Wed - Run the production site continuously for 24 hours to ensure high uptime stability. 24/06/2026 24/06/2026 Documentation Thu - Draft presentation slides and compile a demo video presenting the features of the web app. 25/06/2026 25/06/2026 Reference Fri - Compile the final technical report including High-Level Design (HLD) diagrams, deployment guides, used AWS services table, and peer reviews. 26/06/2026 26/06/2026 FCAJ Learning Hub Achievements in Week 10: Packaged the codebase and locked production configurations. Prepared demo media assets and slides for final project reviews. Finalized the project technical report documentation.

title: References
content:
5.11. References 1. Source code and demo Demo Link (Resume Fit): http://resumematching-alb-1223673352.us-east-1.elb.amazonaws.com Source Code Repository: https://github.com/thuanthien-tran/resume-fit 2. AWS services used by the project The project leverages a robust suite of AWS services to deploy the Resume Fit application in a secure, scalable microservices architecture:
Networking: Amazon VPC, Internet Gateway, and NAT Gateways (providing outbound internet access for private subnets securely). Compute: Amazon EC2 (t3.micro) for the backend servers, integrated with an Auto Scaling Group and Launch Template to dynamically adjust capacity based on traffic. Load Balancing: Application Load Balancer (ALB) distributes incoming application traffic across multiple EC2 instances. Database: Amazon RDS PostgreSQL (Single-AZ) stores candidate metadata and system configuration. Storage & Messaging: Amazon S3 (object storage for CV/PDF files) and Amazon SQS (asynchronous queue processing for AI tasks). Security & Monitoring: AWS Secrets Manager for securing database credentials and API keys, alongside Amazon CloudWatch for centralized logging and metrics monitoring. 3. Cost Breakdown Below is the estimated monthly cost analysis for running the system architecture 24/7 (730 hours/month) in the US East (N. Virginia) – us-east-1 region. The estimations are based on AWS On-Demand Pricing.
No AWS Service Configuration Quantity Estimated Monthly Cost (USD) Notes Total (USD) 1 Amazon VPC Public/Private Subnets, Route Tables 1 0.00 Foundational service 0.00 2 Internet Gateway Internet access for VPC 1 0.00 Free of charge 0.00 3 NAT Gateway 730 hours ($0.045/hr) 2 32.85 Excludes data processing fees 65.70 4 Security Group Stateful virtual firewall - 0.00 Free of charge 0.00 5 Amazon EC2 t3.micro Linux ($0.0104/hr) 3 7.59 1 Backend + 2 in ASG 22.77 6 Amazon EBS gp3, 30GB/instance ($0.08/GB) 3 2.40 Attached to EC2s 7.20 7 Launch Template & ASG Auto Scaling Configuration 1 0.00 Free orchestration service 0.00 8 Application Load Balancer Base price ($0.0225/hr) 1 16.43 Excludes LCU usage fees 16.43 9 Target Group Traffic routing for ALB 1 0.00 Free of charge 0.00 10 Amazon RDS db.t3.micro Single-AZ ($0.017/hr) 1 12.41 Running 730 hours 12.41 11 RDS Storage 20 GB gp3 ($0.115/GB-mo) 1 2.30 DB storage volume 2.30 12 Amazon S3 Standard tier (5 GB) 1 0.12 $0.023/GB-mo 0.12 13 Amazon SQS Standard & DLQ 2 0.00 Within Free Tier (<1M reqs) 0.00 14 AWS Secrets Manager $0.40/secret/month 1 0.40 Stores DB password/API keys 0.40 15 AWS Systems Manager Session Manager - 0.00 Free SSH alternative 0.00 16 Amazon CloudWatch Basic Metrics + Logs - 0.00 Within Free Tier limits 0.00 Total 127.33 Note: The actual total cost might vary slightly due to minor Data Transfer Out and ALB LCU charges. However, given the project’s minimal traffic, these are negligible.
Evaluation and Optimization Proposals Total Estimated Monthly Cost: Approximately $127.33 USD. Cost Structure Analysis: The most expensive resource is the NAT Gateway ($65.70, over 51% of the total cost), followed by Amazon EC2 & EBS Compute resources ($29.97, ~23%). Suitability for AWS Credits Limit: With an estimated monthly burn rate of $127.33, this architecture is safely within the $200 AWS Credits limit. There is a comfortable margin of ~$72 to absorb unexpected data transfer costs or temporary auto-scaling bursts. Cost Optimization Strategies (without altering the core architecture): Resource Scheduling: Although estimated for 24/7 usage, configuring the Auto Scaling Group to scale in to a single instance and disabling one NAT Gateway during off-hours (if High Availability is not strictly required outside evaluation periods) can yield massive savings. Reduce EBS Volume Size: Provisioning 30 GB gp3 per t3.micro instance is likely oversized for a stateless web application container. Reducing this to 10 GB or 15 GB will immediately cut storage costs. Implement VPC Endpoints: For services like S3 and Secrets Manager, setting up VPC Gateway or Interface Endpoints allows private EC2 instances to route traffic internally via the AWS backbone. This bypasses the NAT Gateway data processing fees for these specific requests. 4. Development and deployment The development and deployment lifecycle adhered to modern standard practices:
Source code version control was maintained centrally on GitHub: About GitHub The application was containerized using Docker, ensuring environmental consistency from local development machines directly to production EC2 instances. Reference: Docker on AWS Infrastructure deployment was executed via the AWS Console, heavily relying on EC2 User Data within the Launch Template to automatically install dependencies and pull the latest Docker image whenever the Auto Scaling Group scales out. Reference: Run commands on your Linux instance at launch (User Data) Reference: AWS Auto Scaling Groups Reference: Create a Launch Template for an Auto Scaling group 5. Cost and resource cleanup Because this architecture relies on hourly-billed services (especially NAT Gateways, EC2, and ALB), systematic resource cleanup after the demonstration phase is critical to prevent unwanted credit consumption. Resources must be terminated in reverse-dependency order:
Delete the Auto Scaling Group and Launch Template to stop the continuous spawning of new instances. Reference: Delete your Auto Scaling group Delete the Application Load Balancer and its Target Group. Reference: Delete an Application Load Balancer Manually terminate any remaining EC2 instances. Reference: Terminate EC2 Instances Delete the Amazon RDS database instance and its associated automated snapshots. Reference: Deleting a DB instance Delete the NAT Gateways and subsequently release the Elastic IPs. Reference: Delete a NAT gateway Finally, tear down the Internet Gateway, Route Tables, Subnets, and the VPC itself. Reference: Delete your VPC

title: Worklog Week 11
content:
Objectives of Week 11: Design the group Project, analyze functional and non-functional system requirements. Build the overall system architecture and project Workflow on the AWS platform. Complete technical design documentation, AWS architecture diagrams, and detailed Workflow. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date Mon - Kick off the Project design phase with the team.
- Analyze and document Functional Requirements of the system.
- Identify Non-Functional Requirements: performance, security, scalability. 29/06/2026 29/06/2026 Tue - Design the AWS system Architecture Diagram using Draw.io.
- Map AWS services to each layer: Frontend, Backend, Database, AI, Security.
- Assign responsibilities to team members based on functional modules. 30/06/2026 30/06/2026 Wed - Build detailed Workflow (processing flows) for the project’s core features.
- Design data flow from Client → API Gateway → Lambda → Database/AI Bedrock.
- Create Sequence Diagrams for critical use cases. 01/07/2026 01/07/2026 Thu - Finalize the Technical Design Document.
- Consolidate AWS architecture diagrams, Workflow, ERD, and IAM permission tables into the official document.
- Conduct internal team review and revise based on feedback. 02/07/2026 02/07/2026 Fri - Complete Week 11 worklog.
- Prepare content and deployment plan for Week 12 (Demo, testing). 03/07/2026 03/07/2026 Achievements in Week 11: Completed functional and non-functional requirements analysis for the group project. Successfully built the overall AWS system architecture diagram with all processing layers. Designed detailed Workflow for the application’s main data processing flows. Finalized the Technical Design Document including Architecture Diagram, Workflow, ERD, and IAM permissions. Completed Week 11 worklog on schedule.

title: Worklog Week 12
content:
Objectives of Week 12: Deploy the group project Demo, conduct comprehensive testing, and optimize the system. Finalize the personal internship report summarizing the entire 12-week journey. Complete the capstone Project, group report, and conclude the internship program. Tasks to implement this week: Day Task Start Date End Date Mon - Deploy the product Demo to the official AWS environment.
- Verify connectivity between components: Frontend → API Gateway → Lambda → Database → AI Bedrock.
- Document bugs found during demo and prioritize fixes. 06/07/2026 06/07/2026 Tue - Perform Functional Testing on all application features.
- Security testing: review Security Groups, WAF Rules, IAM Permissions.
- System optimization: fine-tune Lambda performance (memory, timeout), optimize database queries. 07/07/2026 07/07/2026 Wed - Finalize the personal internship report: consolidate 12-week worklog, achievements, and lessons learned.
- Update Self-evaluation and Feedback sections.
- Review the entire Hugo report website content before submission. 08/07/2026 08/07/2026 Thu - Complete the capstone Project: package source code, prepare presentation slides and demo video.
- Write the group summary report: list of deployed AWS services, final architecture diagram, and individual contributions. 09/07/2026 09/07/2026 Fri - Conclude the FCAJ internship program.

- Submit the internship report and capstone Project. 10/07/2026 11/07/2026 Achievements in Week 12: Successfully deployed the product Demo on AWS, all components operating stably. Completed functional and security testing, optimized system performance to meet requirements. Finalized the personal internship report covering all 12 weeks with detailed worklogs. Packaged the capstone Project with presentation slides, demo video, and group report. Concluded and submitted all internship documentation on schedule.
